other_observers: Originally I was alone when I saw the bird; I called Cynthia Madsen who came over and took pictures between 2:30 and 3:30 P.M.; I am the original finder. Originally I identified the bird as a female ( I only had my Sibley Guide available), but others suggested it was a first year male and the discussion on Cobirds with John Drummond and Chris Wood provided confirmation it was a first year male.
duration: Initially less than a minute, but several minutes at a time when it came into the seed I spread out.
specific_location: small footbridge ( I now refer to it as Brambling Bridge) along a path about 20 yards Northeast of the road to the Coyote Crossing Picnic Area (which usually has a bar across it) in the Boat Launch area of Bear Creek Lake Park. North 39deg39'16.2" West 105deg. 08'58.7"
description: Head - sometimes tufted appearance with peak or crest at the rear. Gray head with auricular region outlined in faint black. Narrow black bar just above bill with rest of forehead gray. Finch like bill light yellow with dusky tip. Dark eye on gray cheek. Back of head and down neck two jagged parallel black stripes. Mantle was brown with black edgings on feathers. Underparts were orange/rust with a white belly. The rust was more intense on the shoulders than the breast. Had a few light, black spots on the flanks. Yellowish undertail coverts with notched tail. The wing coverts(as seen in the photos) show orange upper scapulars with a black wing bar (lighter on the outer coverts), then an orange bar with the outer coverts showing some white with fresh orange color on the inners.Behaviors: When I first observed the bird, it was in short grass feeding with some American Tree Sparrows. All I could see was the head, and at first I thought it was a Harris's Sparrow (which had been seen a couple of days earlier in the area), but then I realized the head was gray rather than brown like a winter Harris's Sparrow. As I watched the bird, some of the birds feeding on the ground flew up into a small tree, and when the Brambling flew I could see the orange or rust on the breast and sides. The bird landed on a limb 10-15 up in the small tree along with some of the American Tree Sparrows. Before I could get a definitive look, the birds flew to the northwest.Call: did not hear bird vocalizePlumage: first year male
photographer: Photographer: Mike Henwood, Mike Henwood, Mike Henwood, Mike Henwood, Mike Henwood
